notes: Phase 2a adjudication converts six of the seven previously-disputed items to 'improbable' as written and one (GC-35-017) to the stronger 'discredited,' but five of the seven weakened versions land at well-supported or probable, showing the underlying historical substance mostly survives once the printed absolutes are read as hedged claims rather than categorical assertions. The chapter's sole load-bearing item remains GC-35-057 (Sunday 'originated with Rome'), whose framing_gap of only 2 is the tightest in the batch -- even the strongest minority reconstruction (Bacchiocchi, Pontifical Gregorian UP 1977) cannot lift the weakened claim past 'probable,' confirming this is the chapter's genuine load-bearing weak point rather than a defensible rhetorical simplification. The remaining six items are moderate-to-low centrality supporting texture: individual paragraphs' framing asides, one evidentiary gap about the Sunday-roll forgery's papal provenance reused across GC-35-076 and GC-35-103, and a Waldensian Sabbath-keeping clause overshadowed within the chapter by its own stronger Abyssinian illustration -- none of which the chapter's core 'papal aims are unchanged' argument depends on for its own internal support.

GC-35-017.reasoning: Adjudication rules this discredited as written -- a fact failure: the categorical negation of Catholic Bible access is flatly contradicted by Officiorum ac Munerum (1897), Leo XIII's reading indulgence, and the Society of St. Jerome's mass Gospel distribution, independent documentary counterexamples the ruling treats as decisive. The framing_gap of 4 is the largest in this batch: the weakened claim (conditioned access to church-approved versions only, thin lay Bible culture) survives at well-supported, so the failure is a rhetoric/absoluteness problem layered onto a real underlying restriction, not an invented practice. Within the chapter, paragraph 10 is a passing concessive aside on individual Catholics' sincerity, not one of the chapter's argumentative pillars (papal authority claims, Sunday origin); deleting it removes one explanatory clause but leaves the chapter's papal-aims argument untouched. Independent evidential failure, not inherited from another claim.

GC-35-049.reasoning: A fact failure specific to tense: the 1415 Constance decree (heretics and suspects lose safe-conduct protection) is well-supported and the quoted words substantively track it, but the ruling finds improbable the printed present-tense attribution that the Church still declares this as standing 1911 doctrine, since mainstream Catholic theology from Becanus (1607) onward, and the Catholic Encyclopedia (1908), explicitly denied the maxim. The framing_gap of 3 shows real recovery: the weakened claim (an unrepealed but historically bounded conciliar precedent) is well-supported, so the failure is temporal over-extension dressed in the chapter's 'chameleon/serpent invariance' rhetoric, not fabrication of the underlying episode. Centrality is moderate: paragraph 28 is one of several rhetorical bricks (alongside the independently well-supported Dictatus Papae quotations and episcopal oath) building the 'Rome unchanging' case, so its removal weakens color without collapsing the argument. Independent failure point, no chain inheritance.

GC-35-057.reasoning: This is the chapter's hinge claim, and the fact failure is substantive: the earliest unambiguous first-day witnesses (Barnabas, Ignatius, the Didache) are geographically dispersed and predate or run independent of Rome, so 'originated with Rome' is ruled improbable even after full engagement with the strongest published minority case (Bacchiocchi, Pontifical Gregorian UP 1977). The framing_gap is only 2, the smallest in this batch: the weakened version ('Rome played a leading role in demoting the Sabbath and promoting Sunday') recovers only to probable, not well-supported, so the causal-origin claim loses real ground even after the most charitable reconstruction. Centrality is maximal: paragraphs 35-51 (the Constantine-Eusebius-medieval-legend-Trent excursus, over a third of the chapter) exist to cement this sentence's premise, the direct historical bridge to the book's climactic mark-of-the-beast thesis; delete it and forty paragraphs of illustration support a premise the adjudication itself now finds does not hold as stated. Load-bearing for the chapter's own argument; independent failure, no chain inheritance.

GC-35-076.reasoning: A narrow fact failure: no documentary basis ties the 1201 'Letter from Heaven' roll's production to the pontifical palace -- the contemporary chronicler Roger of Howden records Eustace of Flay's campaign with no papal chain, and the apocryphon itself predates the episode by six centuries. This hardens Andrews's own inference ('responsibility... directly to the pope') into a claim of physical origin the sources do not support, but the framing_gap of 3 shows the underlying phenomenon (a human clerical forgery exploited to enforce Sunday) remains well-supported once the papal-palace specificity is dropped. Centrality is low: the chapter's broader Sunday-origin argument rests on Constantine's edict, Eusebius, and the Trent quotation, not on the precise findspot of one twelfth-century forged letter, so removing this clause leaves the chapter's argument fully intact. Independent evidential failure, unrelated to any other claim's failure.

GC-35-086.reasoning: The persecution of the Waldenses and its use as an illustration of Rome's coercive policy both stand as well-supported; the fact failure is isolated to the existential claim that some Waldenses kept the seventh-day Sabbath, which specialist etymology (insabbatati as footwear, Hoose 2016) and the documented festival-rejecting-but-Sunday-keeping Waldensian record push to improbable. The framing_gap is the smallest in this batch (1): even the narrowed weakened claim (a marginal Bohemian judaizing group, not Waldensians as such) reaches only genuinely-open, resting on one unengaged 15th-century catalog entry, so recovery is thin rather than substantial. Within the chapter this is a single introductory clause before the argument's weight shifts to the far more extensively documented Abyssinian Sabbath-continuity narrative (paragraphs 46-47), so its removal leaves the chapter's 'Rome persecutes Sabbath-keepers' point intact; centrality is nonetheless elevated because Sabbatarian continuity through hidden medieval communities is a recurring pillar feeding the book's central Sabbath-vs-Sunday thesis. Independent failure point; not chain-inherited.

GC-35-103.reasoning: The fact failure is narrow and specific: the anaphoric claim that 'papal leaders... fabricated miracles' to manufacture Sunday's authority has no documentary support, the same evidentiary gap already found in GC-35-076's pontifical-palace claim, since both rest on hardening Andrews's inference about the same forged roll. The other components underlying this sentence (Protestant divine-authority claims, the genuine absence of scriptural warrant, and the shared-evidentiary-gap frame) are well-supported or probable, and the framing_gap of 3 confirms the weakened comparison ('like medieval promoters, Protestants claimed divine authority without a command') is well-supported once the fabrication anaphora is dropped. Centrality is elevated despite the narrow defect because this sentence is the chapter's rhetorical bridge from the historical Sunday-law narrative into paragraph 51's prophetic climax, though it is not load-bearing since the surrounding Revelation-13 argument is framed as theological interpretation independent of this specific historical analogy. Independent evidential failure, not chain-inherited from GC-35-076 despite the shared underlying source problem.

GC-35-110.reasoning: The ruling shows a tied fact-and-frame failure: the political strand (deposing power as an operative 1911 principle) fails as fact -- Vatican I defined nothing on temporal power, Leo XIII framed two distinct supreme orders, no pope of the era asserted deposition -- while the totalizing 'she never changes' identity fails as frame by collapsing real ecclesiological continuity (infallibility, supremacy) together with a defunct political program. The framing_gap of 3 shows real recovery: the weakened claim (Rome never repudiated the medieval claims and reasserted church-over-state doctrine via the Syllabus and Immortale Dei) is well-supported, so the as-written failure is an overstatement of continuity, not invention. Centrality is high because the sentence essentially restates the chapter's own 'aims of the papacy' thesis, but it is not load-bearing: the chapter already establishes Rome's persisting infallibility and coercive doctrine through independently supported material (the Dictatus Papae quotations, the episcopal oath, Pius IX's encyclicals), making this specific Gregory-VII/Innocent-III formulation redundant rather than foundational. Independent evidential failure, no chain inheritance.
